content:
# Acts Chapter 28 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 260-261 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_028_page_001.jpg, ACT_028_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Once we had made it safely to shore, we learned that the island was called Malta.

**2** The local people showed us extraordinary kindness. They lit a fire and welcomed every one of us, because rain was falling and it was cold.

**3** Paul had gathered an armful of brushwood and was laying it on the fire when a viper, driven out by the heat, fastened itself onto his hand.

**4** When the islanders saw the snake hanging from his hand, they said to one another, "This man must be a murderer. He survived the sea, but Justice will not let him live."

**5** But Paul shook the creature off into the fire and was completely unharmed.

**6** They kept watching, expecting him to swell up or suddenly drop dead. But after waiting a long time and seeing nothing unusual happen to him, they changed their minds and began saying he was a god.

**7** Near that place were estates belonging to the leading man of the island, whose name was Publius. He welcomed us and hosted us generously for three days.

**8** It happened that Publius's father was lying sick with fever and dysentery. Paul went in to see him, prayed, placed his hands on him, and healed him.

**9** After that, the rest of the people on the island who had diseases also came and were cured.

**10** They honored us with many gifts, and when we were ready to sail, they loaded us up with everything we needed.

**11** After three months we set sail in an Alexandrian ship that had wintered at the island, one with the Twin Brothers as its figurehead.

**12** We put in at Syracuse and stayed there three days.

**13** From there we followed the coast and arrived at Rhegium. A day later a south wind came up, and on the second day we reached Puteoli.

**14** There we found fellow believers who urged us to stay with them for seven days. And so we came toward Rome.

**15** The brothers and sisters there had heard about us and came out as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns to meet us. When Paul saw them, he thanked God and took courage.

**16** When we arrived in Rome, Paul was allowed to live on his own, with a soldier to guard him.

**17** Three days later, Paul called together the local Jewish leaders. When they had gathered, he said to them, "Brothers, although I have done nothing against our people or the customs of our ancestors, I was handed over to the Romans as a prisoner from Jerusalem.

**18** They examined me and wanted to release me, because there was no basis for a death sentence against me.

**19** But when the Jewish leaders objected, I was forced to appeal to Caesar — not because I had any charge to bring against my own nation.

**20** This is the reason I asked to see you and speak with you: it is because of the hope of Israel that I am bound with this chain."

**21** They replied, "We have received no letters from Judea about you, and none of the brothers who have come from there has reported or said anything bad about you.

**22** But we would like to hear from you what you think, because all we know about this sect is that it is spoken against everywhere."

**23** So they arranged a day and came to him at his lodging in greater numbers. From morning until evening he explained everything to them, testifying about the kingdom of God and trying to persuade them about Jesus from both the Law of Moses and the Prophets.

**24** Some were persuaded by what he said, but others refused to believe.

**25** They disagreed among themselves and began to leave, after Paul had spoken one final word: "The Holy Spirit spoke the truth to your ancestors through the prophet Isaiah when he said,

**26** 'Go to this people and say: You will hear and hear but never understand; you will look and look but never see.

**27** For the heart of this people has grown dull, their ears are hard of hearing, and they have shut their eyes — so that they might not see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their heart, and turn back, and I would heal them.'

**28** "Therefore, let it be known to you that this salvation from God has been sent to the Gentiles — and they will listen."

**29** [After he said this, the Jews left, arguing intensely among themselves.]

**30** Paul lived there for two full years in his own rented quarters, and he welcomed everyone who came to see him,

**31** proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with complete boldness and without any hindrance.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 — "extraordinary kindness":** The Greek (philanthropian) literally means "love of humanity." Rather than the stiff "unusual kindness" of many translations, I chose "extraordinary kindness" to capture the warmth and surprise of the moment — shipwreck survivors being met with genuine human compassion from strangers.

**Verse 4 — "Justice will not let him live":** The Sinaiticus text uses "dike" (Justice/divine retribution), personified. The islanders are invoking a concept of cosmic moral order. I capitalized "Justice" to preserve the sense that they see fate itself as an active force pursuing this man.

**Verse 6 — "changed their minds and began saying he was a god":** The Greek captures a dramatic reversal — from "murderer" to "god" in moments. I rendered this to emphasize the speed of their flip, which Luke clearly finds striking.

**Verse 11 — "the Twin Brothers as its figurehead":** Literally "the Dioscuri" (Castor and Pollux). Since modern English readers would not recognize this term, I rendered the meaning — it was a ship's figurehead depicting the twin gods who were patrons of sailors.

**Verse 14 — "And so we came toward Rome":** This brief, almost understated sentence carries enormous narrative weight — the culmination of Paul's long journey. I kept the simplicity rather than inflating it, letting the understated arrival mirror Luke's style.

**Verse 20 — "the hope of Israel":** Paul is referring to the resurrection and the messianic promise. I preserved the phrase as-is because it functions as a bridge concept — meaningful to both Jewish and Christian readers without requiring explanation.

**Verse 27 — Isaiah 6:9-10 quotation:** I rendered the prophetic quotation in a rhythm that conveys the devastating irony Isaiah intended: repetitive parallelism ("hear and hear... look and look") that mimics the frustrating cycle of encountering truth without receiving it.

**Verse 29:** This verse is present in some manuscript traditions but is considered a later addition. The Sinaiticus text is somewhat ambiguous at this juncture. I have included it in brackets to signal its disputed status, as the content is consistent with the narrative flow.

**Verse 31 — "with complete boldness and without any hindrance":** These are the final words of Acts. Luke ends not with Paul's fate, but with two thundering words in Greek: "with all boldness, unhindered" (meta pases parresias akolutos). The book closes on the unstoppable momentum of the message itself. I expanded slightly to let English carry the same force that the two Greek words deliver as a closing crescendo.

**General note on Acts 28:** This is the final chapter of Luke's two-volume work. The narrative arc moves from shipwreck to salvation, from Malta's kindness to Rome's mixed reception, and closes with Paul preaching freely in the heart of the empire. Luke's genius is in the ending — not a resolution, but an open door. The gospel is loose in the world, and nothing can stop it. My translation choices throughout aim to let that momentum carry through to the last word.

— PNEUMA
